name: juxtorpus

metadata.name: juxtorpus
metadata.summary: Juxtorpus compare and contrast two ATAP corpus.

documentation:
# juxtorpus

Compare and analyse two corpus.

To run this notebook, click on the button below to launch a free binder instance.

DH Day Workshop (14.Mar.23): [![Binder](https://binderhub.atap-binder.cloud.edu.au/badge_logo.svg)](https://binderhub.atap-binder.cloud.edu.au/v2/gh/Sydney-Informatics-Hub/juxtorpus/53ed632722a8a1be5af4547a6965e4b9aa1dfb85?labpath=notebooks%2FDH%20demo%2FDemo-final.ipynb)

documentation:
Similarity between 2 Corpus.

1. jaccard similarity
2. pca similarity

documentation: Return a similarity score between the 2 corpus.

methods.name: juxtorpus.features.similarity.Similarity.lsa_pairwise_cosine
documentation:
Decompose DTM to SVD and return the pairwise cosine similarity of the right singular matrix.

Note: this may be different to the typical configuration using a TDM instead of DTM.
However, sklearn only exposes the right singular matrix.
tdm.T = (U Sigma V.T).T = V.T.T Sigma.T U.T = V Sigma U.T
the term-topic matrix of U is now the right singular matrix if we use DTM instead of TDM.

modules.name: juxtorpus.features.zipf
documentation:
Zipf
The Zipf's Law is an empirical law that says the rank-frequency distribution is an inverse
relation.

It first came about in quantitative linguistics, where given a corpus, the frequency of
any word is inversely proportional to its rank in the frequency table.
i.e. the second most frequent word is half of the most frequent word.

methods.name: juxtorpus.features.zipf.Zipf.set_top
documentation: Set top n words to show.

methods.name: juxtorpus.features.zipf.Zipf.remove_words
documentation: Allow for words to be temporarily removed from your plot without affecting the original.

classes.name: juxtorpus.jux.Jux

documentation: Extract and return the keywords of the two corpus ranked by frequency.

methods.name: juxtorpus.jux.Jux.lexical_diversity
documentation:
Return the lexical diversity comparison.

A smaller corpus will generally have higher lexical diversity.

Jux
This is the main class for Juxtorpus. It takes in 2 corpus and exposes numerous functions
to help contrast the two corpus.

It is expected that the exposed functions are used as tools that serve as building blocks
for your own further analysis.

Set the document frequency range you want to include in the term frequencies.
The values used here is normalised with the number of documents in the corpus.

min_: value between 0.0 and max_.
max_: value between min_ and 1.0.

modules.name: juxtorpus.features.polarity
documentation:
Polarity

This module handles the calculations of polarity of terms based on a chosen metric.

Metrics:
1. term frequency (normalised on total terms)
2. tfidf
3. log likelihood

Output:
-> dataframe

classes.name: juxtorpus.features.polarity.Polarity

methods.name: juxtorpus.features.polarity.Polarity.tf
documentation:
Uses the term frequency to produce the polarity score.

Polarity = Corpus 0's tf - Corpus 1's tf.

methods.name: juxtorpus.features.polarity.Polarity.tfidf
documentation:
Uses the tfidf scores to produce the polarity score.

Polarity = Corpus 0's tfidf - Corpus 1's tfidf.

methods.name: juxtorpus.features.polarity.Polarity.wordcloud
documentation: Generate a wordcloud using one of the 3 modes tf, tfidf, log_likelihood.

Polarity
Gives a 'polarity' score of the two corpus based on token statistics.
A polarity score uses 0 as the midline, a positive score means corpus 0 is dominant. And vice versa.
e.g. tf would use the term frequencies to use as the polarity score.

Each function will return a dataframe with a 'polarity' column and other columns with values that composes
the 'polarity' score.

documentation: Return the annotation when mouse is hovered over the series.

ItemTimeline
This visualisation class plots a number of items and their associated metric across a timeline.
The two modes for the metrics are:
    1. Peak
    2. Cumulative

The top items have maximum opacity 1.0 and then opacity drops.

Factory Methods:
from_freqtables - uses term as items, frequencies as metric.
from_corpus_groups - uses datetime groupings
from_corpus - tries to find a datetime meta and group it (default freq = 1 week)

methods.name: juxtorpus.viz.polarity_wordcloud.PolarityWordCloud._min_max_normalise
documentation:
Normalises the values of the relative scores to be between 1 and 2 from -X to 0 to +X.
where X can be any value dependent the scores the class is initiated with.

Taking values with a midpoint of 0 (relative scores), it first normalises the scores between 0 and 1.
Then move it to between 1 and 2 by summing with 1.

The maximum absolute value of the column is taken as the boundaries. This ensures original scores of 0
are maintained as the midpoint (now 1.5) of the normalised scores i.e. 0 -> 1.5.

Please note: since this converts from negative scale, the 'magnitude' in the abstract sense actually increases
from 1.5 -> 1.0. Then 1.5 -> 2.0. So 1.0 is the maximum polarity for corpus A and 2.0 for corpus B. 1.5 is mid.

methods.name: juxtorpus.viz.polarity_wordcloud.PolarityWordCloud.__invert_normalised_score_to_percentage
documentation:
Converts score in range 1.0 and 2.0 to 0 and 1.0.
 Where 1.5 -> 1 is increasing, so 1.5 -> 0 and 1 -> 1.
 Where 1.501 -> 2 is increasing, so 1.501 -> 0 and 2 -> 1.
(notice it is not 1 -> 1.5 increasing as you would think normally.)

This is used in conjunction with _add_normalised_score as it takes into account the moved axis (+ 1).
Used for gradate()

methods.name: juxtorpus.viz.polarity_wordcloud.PolarityWordCloud._norm_score_to_hsv_wrapper
documentation: Return to_hsv with a different signature required by wordcloud.

PolarityWordCloud

This class visualises two lists of word and score, and puts them in a wordcloud.
The size of the words are determined by the sum of their scores.
The colour is determined by their relative score.

Usage:
```
# quick method
A = [('hello', 1.0), ('orange', 2.0)]
B = [('hello', 1.0), ('apple', 0.5)]

wc_ = PolarityWordCloud.from_(A, B, top=10)
wc_.gradate().render()
```
# custom
df_A = pd.DataFrame(A, columns=[PolarityWordCloud.COL_WORD, PolarityWordCloud.COL_SCORE])
df_B = pd.DataFrame(B, columns=[PolarityWordCloud.COL_WORD, PolarityWordCloud.COL_SCORE])
wc_ = PolarityWordCloud(WC(background_color='white'), df_A, df_B)
wc_.top(10)
wc_.gradate(scheme='default').render()
```

methods.name: juxtorpus.utils.deduplicated_dir.DeduplicatedDirectory.exists
documentation:
Check if file already exists in the directory.

```
param file

path to file.

param shallow

True - checks file metadata only. False - checks content.
```

Uses filecmp under the hood.

methods.name: juxtorpus.utils.deduplicated_dir.DeduplicatedDirectory.content_exists
documentation:
Check if content exists in directory.
\:param content:
\:param shallow: True - check size only. False - checks content.

The DeduplicatedDirectory is a proxy to a temporary directory that does not hold any duplicated files.

It does this by keeping track of each file's message digest in an index.
